description: Rasi (sign-based) dasha system where each of the 12 zodiac signs becomes a dasha lord in sequence, starting from the Lagna (or Arudha Lagna, per some traditions).

starting_point.rule: The first Mahadasha is of the sign occupied by the Lagna (Ascendant sign). Some traditions use the Arudha Lagna sign instead — flag this as a configurable option.
starting_point.sequence_direction: The overall sequence of 12 Mahadasha signs proceeds in zodiacal (forward) order from the starting sign if the starting sign is odd, and in counter-zodiacal (reverse) order if the starting sign is even.

mahadasha_duration_calculation.step_1: Identify the dispositor (lord) of the sign, and the sign it currently occupies in the birth chart.
mahadasha_duration_calculation.step_2: Count the number of signs (inclusive of both start and end sign) from the dasha sign to the sign occupied by its lord.

direction_of_counting.odd_sign: count zodiacally (forward, e.g. Aries -> Taurus -> Gemini ...)
direction_of_counting.even_sign: count counter-zodiacally (backward, e.g. Taurus -> Aries -> Pisces ...)

mahadasha_duration_calculation.duration_years: The count obtained in step_2 (a number from 1 to 12) is the duration of that sign's Mahadasha in years.

exceptions.case: Lord placed in its own sign (count = 1)
exceptions.rule: Duration is taken as 12 years (full period) instead of 1.

exceptions.case: Aries and Scorpio (both ruled by Mars)
exceptions.rule: Some classical sources (Jaimini sutras) fix these at 7 years each regardless of the counted value, due to Mars owning two signs. This is a commonly cited exception but not universally applied by all software — flag as a configurable/toggleable rule since practitioners differ on this.

exceptions.case: Lord placed in the 12th sign from the dasha sign (in the counting direction)
exceptions.rule: Some texts reduce or adjust duration further; treatment varies by author. Recommend leaving this off unless following a specific named source (e.g., Sanjay Rath's Jaimini method).

mahadasha_duration_calculation.total_cycle_check: Regardless of chart, the sum of all 12 Mahadasha durations in one complete cycle always equals exactly 144 years. Use this as a validation check on the calculation.

antardasha_sub_period_calculation.step_1: List all 12 signs starting from the Mahadasha sign itself, proceeding in the same direction as the overall Mahadasha sequence (forward if Mahadasha sign is odd, backward if even).
antardasha_sub_period_calculation.step_2: For each of these 12 signs, compute its OWN independent full-cycle dasha duration in years (using the same mahadasha_duration_calculation logic applied to that sign, ignoring the current chart's Mahadasha context).
antardasha_sub_period_calculation.step_3: Scale each sign's antardasha length proportionally: antardasha_duration = (Mahadasha_duration_years * that_sign's_own_full_years) / 144
antardasha_sub_period_calculation.notes: This preserves proportional weighting so all 12 antardashas within a Mahadasha sum exactly to the Mahadasha's total duration.

further_sub_levels.pratyantar_dasha: Apply the same proportional_scaling method recursively: within each Antardasha, list 12 signs starting from the Antardasha sign (direction per its own parity), scale by (AD_duration * sign_full_years)/144.
further_sub_levels.sookshma_dasha: Repeat the same recursive method one level deeper for further precision.
further_sub_levels.general_rule: Every sub-level uses the identical recursive algorithm: (parent_duration * child_sign_full_years) / 144, with direction determined by the parity (odd/even) of the current level's starting sign.

known_variations_across_sources: Starting point: Lagna sign vs Arudha Lagna sign (this changes the entire sequence).
Whether Aries/Scorpio 7-year fixed exception is applied.
Whether Rahu/Ketu are ever substituted as co-lords in special chart configurations (rare, mostly ignored in mainstream Chara Dasha).
Some authors count antardasha differently (equal 1/12 division rather than proportional scaling) — proportional scaling is the majority/software-standard approach.
